verdict_reasoning: HMGB1 is a 215-aa nuclear DNA-binding protein with no TM domain, GPI anchor, or covalent surface-anchor signal. It is secreted via leaderless pathway and lysosomal exocytosis, then acts extracellularly as a DAMP molecule binding reversibly to surface receptors (RAGE, TLR4, TLR2). Application of the wash test confirms it does not remain on the cell surface via its own mechanism—it is in equilibrium with the soluble pool. No gene-specific evidence documents a stable or canonical cell-state-induced surface pool. Platelet data hint at possible activation-state display, but lack direct surface-biotinylation or flow-cytometry confirmation on intact cells. Verdict ruled by absence of membrane integration, lack of stable TM-partner anchoring, and reversible receptor binding characteristic of secreted-only proteins.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Platelet/cell-activation biology: One reference suggests activated platelets expose HMGB1 on surface, but mechanism and generalizability to other cell types unclear; no direct surface-biotinylation or live-cell flow data for this protein confirm a canonical cell-state-induced pool. Gene-specific experimental evidence for cell-state-induced ecto-presentation would raise verdict to contextual.
